# Grigorii Moskalev — Legal counsel and legal engineer

> Grigorii Moskalev is a legal counsel at FaceApp who builds AI tools for legal teams. 11 years practicing law, 3 years writing Python. Author of PII Shield (open-source MCP server that anonymizes documents before LLM analysis) and Brand monitor (trademark-infringement scanner). Teaches a course on LLM use for lawyers — 100+ students. Based in Limassol, Cyprus.

This file is the long-form Markdown export of gmoskalev.com for LLM ingestion. The slim index is at [llms.txt](https://gmoskalev.com/llms.txt).

---

# Profile

- **Name:** Grigorii Moskalev (also Greg Moskalev / Grigory Moskalyov)
- **Role:** Legal Counsel @ FaceApp
- **Based:** Limassol, Cyprus
- **Stack:** Law · Python · LLMs
- **Languages:** English, Russian, Greek

# About

11 years practicing law, 3 years writing Python, and a growing overlap between the two. I run the legal function at FaceApp, a consumer product used by hundreds of millions, across IP, privacy, corporate, and app-store disputes. Before that, KPMG and senior in-house roles at international tech companies across Cyprus, the EU, and beyond.

Legal engineering is what I genuinely love. I've built RAG pipelines over our internal corpus, contract analytics, MCP servers that keep client data off the API. I work with prompt engineering and fine-tuning, and I deploy local models when client data shouldn't reach a hosted one in the first place.

I've also shipped legal-tech projects as a product owner, taking ideas from zero to launch and owning the spec, the flows, and the API.

I teach a course and run a Telegram channel for lawyers learning to work with LLMs — 100+ students so far.

## Quick facts

- 11 years practicing law
- Practice areas: IP · Privacy · Corporate
- 100+ students taught
- Legal function lead at FaceApp
- Languages: EN · RU · GR

# Projects

## PII Shield

- **Tagline:** Anonymize documents before Claude sees them. Restore real data after analysis.
- **Status:** Live, v2.0.2
- **Repo:** https://github.com/gregmos/PII-Shield
- **License:** MIT
- **Stack:** Node.js, MCP, GLiNER, ONNX, DOCX

MCP server for Claude Desktop. Reads documents locally, swaps every name, ID, and number for placeholders (`<PERSON_1>`, `<ORG_1>`, etc.), and sends only the anonymized text to Claude. After analysis, the real values are stitched back into the final document — all on your machine. PII never enters the API.

## Brand monitor

- **Tagline:** Find the apps stealing your name and the sites cracking your build — every week, on autopilot.
- **Status:** Live
- **Repo:** https://github.com/gregmos/Brand-monitor
- **Stack:** SKILL.md, iTunes API, Vision, WHOIS, Excel

Skill that scans the App Store and the open web for knockoffs and cracked APKs of your app. iTunes Search across countries plus fuzzy-matching narrows ~1,000 listings to ~50 real candidates; icon vision and WHOIS-backed evidence packages confirm the rest. An LLM layer scores risk, recommends action, and ships a prioritized Excel report. The whole thing is a single SKILL.md — drop it into Claude or OpenClaw, tell it your trademark and target countries, and it runs itself. Hours of manual work → ~20 minutes of review.

# Writing

Notes on legal-AI work — originally posted on LinkedIn. Live index: https://gmoskalev.com/writing

## PII Shield v2 — anonymize the matter, not the document (28 Apr 2026)

10 contracts on your desk. Same counterparties across half of them. You want Claude to analyze them as one matter, without a single client name reaching the Claude API. That's what PII Shield v2 does. v1 worked one file at a time — "Acme Corp" became ORG_1 in one document and could be ORG_3 in another; cross-document analysis broke. v2 holds the matter, not the document: "Acme Corp" stays the same placeholder across all 10 files. Each anonymized file carries the session ID, so open one in 4 weeks, deanonymize, and the real data comes back. Export the matter as an encrypted archive (AES-GCM via scrypt), a colleague imports with the passphrase and continues on their laptop. PII never moves between you and never reaches the API. Entity-review panel now lives inside Claude Desktop, not a separate browser — mark a missed entity once, it propagates across the session; reject a false positive once, it's gone everywhere. Tested on 50 English-language documents: 95% recall, low false positives. One-drop install, no Python. Next: a CLI so PII Shield runs on a folder of contracts from any tool, not just Claude. (Source: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/grigorii-moskalev_pii-shield-v2-ugcPost-7454803663107096576-oGbO)

## Stop shipping memos as 14-page .docx (21 Apr 2026)

Honest question to legal colleagues: aren't you tired of writing boring memos? Tech founders open a 14-page .docx in Times New Roman, see numbered paragraphs and a nine-line "Executive Summary," and ask privately: could you just tell me what this means? With Claude's design tool I turned the Duke Memo into a web page in a single prompt — headings that breathe, sections you can navigate, citations that look like citations. Sometimes you ship both: the web version for the client to actually read, the document version for the file. Clients who enjoy reading your work tend to come back for more. (Source: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/grigorii-moskalev_honest-question-for-my-legal-colleagues-share-7452308266916884480-pMAQ)

## PII Shield + Human-in-the-Loop review (Apr 2026)

Two days after I shipped PII Shield (500+ reactions, 80+ comments) I spent a night building what people asked for. HITL review: a local web UI showing every detected entity color-coded in context — click to remove false positives, select text to add missed ones; all matching occurrences update automatically; Claude re-anonymizes with corrections, override data never touches the API. Plus an audit log of every MCP call (proof for compliance teams that only file paths and session IDs flow through the API) and an NER log of every detection decision, both on-disk under ~/.pii_shield/audit/. (Source: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/grigorii-moskalev_two-days-ago-i-shared-pii-shield-local-ugcPost-7445484144651984897-PCSE)

## Claude Cowork that never sees your client data (Apr 2026)

PII Shield is a Cowork Skill paired with a local MCP server. Connect a folder, type "write a risk memo for vendor_agreement.docx" — anonymization happens locally before Claude sees a thing. "John Smith" becomes <PERSON_1>, "Acme Corp" becomes <ORG_1>. Claude analyzes the anonymized version; PII Shield restores everything in the final .docx with the formatting you prefer. Detection runs on a GLiNER NER model plus custom recognizers; 90%+ of PII gets replaced automatically. Important nuance: connect documents as a folder, don't drag-attach to the message — attached files get rendered before tools run.

## An AI agent that follows citation chains (Mar 2026)

An AI agent opened a case-law database, ran a search, screened 24 results, picked the four that mattered, followed their citation chains (ILVA A/S → Deutsche Wohnen → Nacionalinis → TR v Land Hessen), and delivered a structured digest. I made coffee. Not keyword matching — actual research behavior. Claude read all four CJEU rulings (60-100 paragraphs each) plus the AG opinions, came back with a table mapping how the proportionality doctrine developed across all four. Claude Max ($100/mo) holds the thread across long legal texts; Pro loses context after ~40 paragraphs. Output isn't perfect — fifteen minutes of editing versus half a day reading and taking notes. (Source: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/grigorii-moskalev_an-ai-agent-opened-a-case-law-database-ran-activity-7441766600648810496-E6py)

## One memo and a Claude subscription (Mar 2026)

I took our strongest memo and fed it to Claude's Skill creation. It parsed the .docx XML, figured out fonts, spacing, heading hierarchy, quote indentation, risk-assessment layout — turned it into a reusable SKILL.md. Now I throw raw notes from a call at it and get back a properly-formatted memo: numbered sections, citations in original language, risk assessments per issue, actual .docx not markdown. Reproduces Roboto 12pt, 11pt italic quotes with left indent, justified, single spacing. Also matches my writing style and terminology. Took 20 minutes to set up. Works for memos, contracts, claims, policies, board resolutions. I still do the legal analysis. I just stopped doing the formatting. (Source: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/grigorii-moskalev_one-memo-and-a-claude-subscription-thats-activity-7440289317366784000-yyQQ)

## Brand monitor — weekly trademark hunt (Mar 2026)

I deployed an AI agent that finds trademark infringers and pirated app builds every week. Two tracks: App Store — queries iTunes Search across countries, fuzzy-matches names and bundle IDs against registered trademarks, narrows ~1,000 listings to ~50 candidates, runs icon vision on the high-risk ones. Piracy — searches mod/cracked APK sites, parses download pages, collects evidence packages (screenshots, HTML, WHOIS) for confirmed violations. LLM layer scores risk, recommends action, ships a prioritized Excel report. Hours of manual work → ~20 minutes reviewing. Single SKILL.md file — drop into Claude or OpenClaw with your trademark and target countries. Repo: https://github.com/gregmos/Brand-monitor (Source: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/grigorii-moskalev_i-deployed-an-openclaw-ai-agent-to-find-trademark-activity-7439559446172835840-oP8n)

For anything at the intersection of law and AI — let's talk.

---

# PII Shield — full product page

## What it is

PII Shield is an open-source MCP server for Claude Desktop. The user drops a file into a connected folder; PII Shield reads it locally, replaces every name, ID, and number with placeholders, and only the anonymized text crosses the wire. After the model finishes, the real values are stitched back into the output on the user's machine. The original text never leaves the disk.

## Pipeline (how it flows)

The document is anonymized locally; only placeholder text reaches Claude. When the model is done, PII is stitched back in — without ever leaving your disk.

Stages:
1. **Document** (PDF · DOCX · TXT) — has PII
2. **PII Shield** — anonymize (LOCAL)
3. **Claude API** — analyze placeholders (REMOTE)
4. **PII Shield** — restore (LOCAL)
5. **Result** — analysis with real names

PII never leaves the machine. Anonymization and restoration both happen locally.

### MCP call sequence (developer view)

- Document → Shield: `anonymize_file({ file_path, session_id? })` → `{ session_id, entity_count, output_path, … }`
- Shield → Claude API: Claude reads `output_path` from the connected folder. Only placeholders cross the wire.
- Claude API → Shield: model output flows back as text — still placeholder-only.
- Shield → Result: `deanonymize_docx({ file_path, session_id })` or `deanonymize_text({ text, session_id })` stitches the real PII back in, locally.

### Transform example (before/after Claude sees)

Before (real document):
- John Smith signed the NDA.
- ACME Corp v. Globex.
- Contact: john@acme.example
- Amount: €120,000 via IBAN CY17…
- UK NIN: AB123456C · DOB 1984-06-12

After (what Claude sees over API):
- `<PERSON_1>` signed the NDA.
- `<ORG_1>` v. `<ORG_2>`.
- Contact: `<EMAIL_1>`
- Amount: `<MONEY_1>` via IBAN `<IBAN_1>`
- UK NIN: `<UK_NIN_1>` · DOB `<DATE_1>`

## Why this exists

Pasting client material into a consumer LLM is both a privacy and a privilege problem.

A federal court has already confirmed the privilege side directly: in *United States v. Heppner* (S.D.N.Y., February 2026), documents a defendant created with consumer Claude were ruled outside attorney-client privilege. An AI tool is not an attorney and owes no duty of confidentiality.

PII Shield runs the redaction step before the model sees anything. It's an MCP server for Claude Desktop — drop a file, Claude receives placeholders like `<PERSON_1>` and `<ORG_2>`, and the real values are stitched back into the output on your machine. The original text never leaves your disk.

### v1 → v2 · the rewrite (developer view)

v1 was Python + Presidio + spaCy — every install fought Claude Desktop's bundled runtime. v2.0.2 is a complete Node.js rewrite over `onnxruntime-node` + `@xenova/transformers`: same detection coverage, no Python.

Plugin: 700 KB on Windows/Linux (host Node), or 83 MB on macOS (bundled Node 24.15.0). The 634 MB `gliner-pii-base-v1.0` model installs on-demand via an in-chat panel — never bundled in the artefact. CI runs the matrix on Ubuntu/Windows/macOS × Node 18/20.

Both v1.0.0 and v2.0.2 release tags live in `gregmos/PII-Shield` — the v1 Python build remains downloadable for legacy installs.

## Skill modes (six task-shaped workflows)

The companion `pii-contract-analyze` skill ships six task-shaped modes. Each runs the same anonymize → analyze → deanonymize pipeline, just optimized for a different deliverable:

1. **MEMO** — legal memorandum on a contract or set of contracts
2. **REDLINE** — tracked-changes redline against a baseline document
3. **SUMMARY** — short-form summary
4. **COMPARISON** — diff two documents
5. **BULK** — process up to five files in one session
6. **ANONYMIZE** — anonymize without analysis (handoff use)

## What it detects

17 country-specific patterns on top of generic name and entity detection.

### Jurisdictions (17 EU + UK patterns)

- **United Kingdom** (5): NIN · NHS · Passport · CRN · Driving licence
- **Germany** (2): Tax ID · Social security
- **France** (2): NIR · CNI
- **Italy** (2): Fiscal code · VAT
- **Spain** (2): DNI · NIE
- **Cyprus** (2): TIC · National ID
- **EU-wide** (2): VAT · Passport

### Generic, jurisdiction-agnostic

person · org · location · email · phone · IBAN · credit card · crypto wallet · US SSN · US EIN · medical licence · money · date

### Detection method (developer view)

- **NER (zero-shot)** via GLiNER: person, org, location
- **Regex / pattern**: all 17 jurisdiction patterns + email · phone · IBAN · credit card · crypto · SSN · EIN · licences · money · date

## Quick start

Four steps. No terminal.

1. **Download the artefacts** — pick the `.mcpb` for your OS plus the contract-analyze skill. Direct links to the v2.0.2 release: https://github.com/gregmos/PII-Shield/releases/tag/v2.0.2
2. **Install in Claude Desktop** — Settings → Extensions → Advanced Settings → Install extension. On first call, PII Shield runs `npm ci --ignore-scripts` to install pinned runtime deps (`onnxruntime-node`, `@xenova/transformers`, `gliner`) into `~/.pii_shield/deps/` — 2-3 minutes once per machine, instant thereafter.
3. **Connect a folder** with your documents — Claude Desktop → Settings → Connected folders. (Don't drag-attach files into the chat — the raw document hits the API before PII Shield can intercept.)
4. **Use it from a chat** — pick the `pii-contract-analyze` skill, tell Claude what you need.

### First-run model install

The first time you ask Claude to anonymize, PII Shield notices the GLiNER model isn't on disk and opens an in-chat install panel: download (~634 MB, fetched by browser) and install ZIP (validated, atomic-extracted into `~/.pii_shield/models/`). Subsequent runs skip the panel.

### Build & verify locally (developer)

```
npm ci --ignore-scripts --legacy-peer-deps
npm run build              # esbuild bundle
npm run smoke              # node scripts/smoke-protocol.mjs
npm test                   # 8 suites, incl. multi-doc HITL & session archival
```

CI runs the same matrix on Ubuntu / Windows / macOS × Node 18 / 20.

## Compliance & audit

Three logs, all on-disk:

- `~/.pii_shield/audit/mcp_audit.log` — every MCP call (anonymize, deanonymize, export, import) with timestamp, file path, entity counts.
- `~/.pii_shield/audit/ner_init.log` — model initialization events.
- `~/.pii_shield/audit/pii_shield_server.log` — server lifecycle.

No telemetry, no remote logging endpoint. If your DPO asks "what did the tool do with that file at 14:02 UTC," the answer is on your filesystem.

### Storage layout (developer)

Encrypted handoff: `export_session(passphrase)` → `.pii-session` archive (AES-GCM + scrypt-derived key). Mappings live at `~/.pii_shield/mappings/`, models at `~/.pii_shield/models/`, audit trail appendable at `~/.pii_shield/audit/mcp_audit.log`. Session TTL 7 days, configurable. All inputs validated with Zod (TypeScript strict). Off-network by design — no telemetry endpoint to disable.

## Features

- **Zero PII in API** — every call to Claude carries placeholders only.
- **GLiNER NER** — `gliner-pii-base-v1.0` running on `onnxruntime-node`, on-device.
- **HITL review** — `start_review` opens an in-chat iframe to add/remove/edit detected entities; `apply_review_overrides` propagates corrections across all occurrences in the session.
- **Entity dedup** — identical entities collapse to one placeholder; reduces output token noise.
- **Cross-session deanon** — `deanonymize_docx` works in any chat using the docx's embedded session_id.
- **Multi-file sessions** — pass `session_id` to `anonymize_file` to extend a session; identical entities across files share placeholders.
- **Encrypted handoff** — `export_session(passphrase)` → `.pii-session` archive (AES-GCM via scrypt). Hand off to a colleague with just the passphrase.
- **On-device audit logging** — every action recorded to `~/.pii_shield/audit/mcp_audit.log`.

## MCP tools (developer view — 17 tools, 4 groups)

### Anonymize (4)
- `anonymize_file` — preferred entry point. Anonymizes PDF/DOCX/TXT. PII stays on host.
- `anonymize_text` — anonymize raw string.
- `anonymize_docx` — DOCX-specific, preserves formatting.
- `anonymize_next_chunk` — chunked processing for documents >5000 chars.

### Review (5)
- `start_review` — open the HITL iframe inside Claude Desktop.
- `apply_review_overrides` — apply user edits, propagate across the session.
- `get_full_anonymized_text` — return full anonymized text for piping.
- `deanonymize_text` — restore PII placeholders → real values in a string.
- `deanonymize_docx` — restore PII in DOCX using its embedded `session_id`.

### Session (3)
- `export_session` — pack mapping + anonymized files into encrypted `.pii-session` archive.
- `import_session` — unlock and load a `.pii-session` archive.
- `get_mapping` — inspect the placeholder ↔ value mapping.

### Utility (5)
- `scan_text` — preview-only entity detection without creating a session.
- `list_entities` — list all detected entities, grouped by type.
- `find_file` — resolve a path inside a connected folder.
- `resolve_path` — relative or absolute path resolution.
- `install_model_from_download` — install GLiNER from a local download.

### Round-trip example

```
// 1. anonymize a file — original stays on disk; only placeholders cross the wire
→ anonymize_file({ file_path: "~/contracts/nda.docx" })
← { status: "success",
    session_id: "m9q8x4-7b5a91",
    entity_count: 14,
    output_path: ".../nda.anon.txt",
    docx_output_path: ".../nda.anon.docx",
    by_type: { PERSON: 4, ORG: 3, MONEY: 2, ... } }

// 2. Claude reads output_path and drafts a memo using placeholders only

// 3. restore PII into the draft, locally
→ deanonymize_text({ text: draft, session_id: "m9q8x4-7b5a91" })
← { status: "success",
    deanonymized_text: "...John Smith signed the NDA..." }
```

All inputs validated with Zod. Errors return MCP-protocol-shaped `{ isError: true, content: [...] }` instead of throwing.

## Stack

Built with: Node.js 18+, MCP (Model Context Protocol), GLiNER, onnxruntime-node, @xenova/transformers, DOCX (pure JS), pdf-parse, AES-GCM / scrypt, MIT license.

Tested on: Ubuntu / Windows / macOS, Node 18 / 20, 8 test suites, MCP protocol smoke test, Zod schema validation.
